input.prompt:
You are Teammate 1 — the Data Analyst — in a 9-agent team building a data acquisition strategy for Bonamoussadi, Douala, Cameroon.

Your job: Research the local context for these 7 verticals and produce a structured report. Use web search extensively.

VERTICALS: billboards, alcohol, census, fuel, mobile money, pharmacy, transport roads (including blocked roads)

For EACH vertical, research and document:

1. **Existing data sources** — What structured datasets exist today? (government, NGO, private sector, open data portals, OSM, satellite imagery providers, telecom data, etc.)
2. **Field realities in Bonamoussadi/Douala** — What does the ground truth look like? Density of businesses, informal vs formal economy split, local languages, accessibility, infrastructure quality.
3. **Update frequency** — How often does this data change? (billboards rotate monthly? fuel prices change weekly? pharmacies open/close quarterly?)
4. **Data quality risks** — What are the main risks: outdated records, informal businesses not registered, GPS accuracy issues in dense urban areas, seasonal variations, political sensitivities (census), etc.
5. **Key local actors** — Who collects, owns, or brokers this data locally? (Mairie de Douala, INS Cameroun, MINCOMMERCE, telecom operators like MTN/Orange, local advertising agencies, etc.)
6. **Gaps and opportunities** — What data is simply not collected today? Where is the biggest opportunity for a new data layer?

Also research:
- Cameroon's data protection law (Loi n°2010/012) and any recent updates
- Mobile penetration rates and smartphone adoption in Douala
- Internet connectivity quality in Bonamoussadi
- Existing geospatial/mapping initiatives (OSM coverage, Google Maps completeness, what's missing)
- Local competitors or similar data collection efforts

Format your output as a clean, comprehensive report with clear sections per vertical plus a cross-cutting section on infrastructure and regulatory context. Be specific — cite actual organizations, laws, datasets, and URLs where possible.

OUTPUT THIS AS A COMPLETE REPORT — do not write files. Just return the full text.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Cameroon's data protection law framework:

## Law 2010/012 and Personal Data Protection

Law No. 2010/012 of December 21, 2010 relates to cybersecurity and cybercrime in Cameroon. This law sets out provisions on the protection of individuals' privacy, data retention periods, and electronic communications confidentiality.

## New Comprehensive Data Protection Law (2024)

In late 2024, Cameroon enacted new data protection legislation. On 23 December 2024, Cameroon became the 38th African country to have enacted comprehensive legislation on data protection, with Law No. 2024/017 Relating to Personal Data Protection in Cameroon.

The Act covers several GDPR aspects of data protection, such as the portability right, the obligation to conduct impact assessments, or the obligation to keep records of processing. Additionally, the Act provides for the creation of a supervisory authority named the Personal Data Protection Authority.

## Compliance Deadline

Cameroon adopted Law no.2024/017 on December 23, 2024, which granted companies a deadline for aligning their data processing protocols with legal requirements, with this deadline expiring on 23 June 2026.

Law 2010/012 remains relevant as complementary legislation that works alongside the newer comprehensive data protection law.

## Moto-Taxi Activity Data

### Forest and Environmental Monitoring
Participatory 3D Mapping projects have been conducted in communities surrounding the Tofala-Mone East Wildlife Corridor in South West Cameroon by the African Conservation Foundation and Environment and Rural Development Foundation, with the goal of establishing effective community forest monitoring by integrating Global Forest Watch data and Participatory 3-Dimensional Mapping.

Geographical Information Systems and Remote Sensing techniques are key to ensuring a better understanding of coastal areas in Cameroon, with these technologies providing policy makers and environmental actors a better picture of the evolution of coastal zones over the years.

### Cocoa Mapping and Sustainability
As the world's fifth-largest cocoa producer, Cameroon is reinforcing its strategic position by launching an ambitious national mapping of its cocoa plantations as part of the EU Sustainable Cocoa Programme. The project leverages artificial intelligence and satellite data to identify production areas and assess the effects of cocoa cultivation on forest ecosystems.

### Forest Protection through Satellite Monitoring
Cameroon has joined a Congo Basin initiative that uses satellite imagery to monitor changes in forest cover, gaining a license to use images from the SPOT satellite earth observation system which could assist in protecting its rich forest reserve.

### Geodetic Network Development
A national geodetic network provides an objective, scientific basis for applications including measuring and delineating property parcels and building cadastral maps, giving Cameroon land property information critical to the nation's legal and fiscal system.

LOCAL MUNICIPALITY BY-LAWS FOR THE CONTROL OF OUTDOOR ADVERTISING","url":"https://cogta.mpg.gov.za/policies/ByLaws/OutdoorAdvertByLaw.pdf"},{"title":"236.16 Outdoor Advertising - Engineering Policy Guide","url":"https://epg.modot.org/index.php/236.16_Outdoor_Advertising"},{"title":"8 Outdoor Advertising Rules & Regulations Every Advertiser Should Know | billups OOH Insights","url":"https://www.billups.com/articles/8-outdoor-advertising-rules-regulations-every-advertiser-should-know"},{"title":"ekurhuleni metropolitan municipality billboards and the ...","url":"https://www.ekurhuleni.gov.za/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/01_BillboardsDisplayAdvertisementsByLaws.pdf"},{"title":"SCDOT Outdoor Advertising New and Existing Permits and Laws, Portal","url":"https://scdot.org/business/outdoor-advertising.aspx"},{"title":"Outdoor Advertising & Signage Policy | Nairobi City County","url":"https://nairobi.go.ke/download/outdoor-advertising-signage-policy"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Cameroon's outdoor advertising regulations:

## Key Regulatory Framework

Payment of advertising rights is regulated by the law of December 29, 2006, which governs advertising in Cameroon. All advertising must be subject to taxes and fees for the purchase of advertising space (Article 20) and must be authorized (Article 21).

## Roles of MINCOM and Municipalities

MINCOM's mission is to authorize individuals to become advertisers by issuing permits, but even with a permit, the municipality must grant space for the installation. Municipalities are responsible for authorizing the installation of billboards because they manage urban areas.

## Authorization and Permits

To advertise, you need an approved agency recognized by the CUY (Urban Community of Yaoundé) and MINCOM, and without ministry approval, the city can't endorse any advertising agency. The advertising agent acts between the municipality, which grants the advertising space, and the advertiser, to whom it subleases this space.

## Fees and Taxation

Any individual or entity broadcasting advertisements must obtain authorization from the competent authority and compensate accordingly. Companies argue that the fee collected by decentralized territorial collectivities (CTDs) is unfounded.

## Enforcement

Illegal advertising occurs when you place an advertisement without authorization. If a billboard is displayed without meeting these conditions, it can be considered fraudulent.

Now I have enough data to compile the comprehensive report. Here it is:

---

# DATA ACQUISITION STRATEGY: BONAMOUSSADI, DOUALA, CAMEROON
## Local Context Research Report -- 7 Verticals

---

## CROSS-CUTTING CONTEXT: INFRASTRUCTURE, REGULATORY, AND DIGITAL LANDSCAPE

### Geographic and Demographic Context

Bonamoussadi is located within the **Douala V** (Douala 5eme) arrondissement of Douala, Cameroon's economic capital. Douala's metro population reached approximately **4.35 million** in 2025 (a 3.4% annual increase). The city is divided into six communes d'arrondissement, managed by the **Communaute Urbaine de Douala (CUD)**, which covers 923 km2 with 21,000 hectares allocated to housing, commercial, and industrial uses.

Bonamoussadi was historically a residential neighborhood but has experienced rapid commercialization, with malls, supermarkets, restaurants, and nightclubs proliferating in recent years. It is one of Douala's more middle-class and upper-middle-class areas, making it both denser and more formalized than many other Douala neighborhoods -- though informality remains pervasive.

**Language**: Douala is primarily francophone. In 2014, 76.4% of Douala inhabitants over 15 spoke and understood French. English is also an official language but secondary here. Local languages (Duala, Bassa, Bamileke dialects) are common in daily commerce.

### Mobile Penetration and Smartphone Adoption

- Mobile connections in Cameroon reached **96.4% of total population** (October 2025), with **31.5 million active subscriptions** -- up 15.8% in 2024.
- **87.5%** of mobile connections are broadband (3G/4G/5G).
- **12.6 million** internet users (41.9% penetration) as of end 2025.
- Sub-Saharan Africa smartphone adoption stood at ~55% in 2023; Cameroon is likely in the 45-55% range.
- Market share: **Orange Cameroon 50.08%**, **MTN Cameroon 47.2%**, **CAMTEL 2.67%**.

Sources: [DataReportal Digital 2026: Cameroon](https://datareportal.com/reports/digital-2026-cameroon), [TechAfrica News on telecom growth](https://techafricanews.com/2025/12/09/cameroons-telecom-sector-grows-18-in-2024-driven-by-mobile-and-digital-services/)

### Internet Connectivity in Bonamoussadi/Douala

- Orange and MTN provide 3G/4G coverage across Douala. Coverage maps available at [nPerf.com](https://www.nperf.com/en/map/CM/2232593.Douala/-./signal).
- CAMTEL provides fixed broadband via fiber optic in select areas. CAMTEL coverage map: [camtel.cm/coverage](https://camtel.cm/coverage).
- A DOUALA-IX internet exchange point exists, enhancing regional connectivity ([douala-ix.net](https://www.douala-ix.net/)).
- 5G deployment is beginning at the Port of Douala-Bonaberi, but consumer 5G is not yet widespread.
- **Reality check**: In dense urban neighborhoods like Bonamoussadi, 4G speeds can be inconsistent due to congestion, especially during peak hours. Power outages (frequent in Cameroon) further disrupt connectivity.

### Cameroon Data Protection Law

**Old regime**: Law No. 2010/012 of December 21, 2010 on Cybersecurity and Cybercrime, which included provisions on privacy and data retention.

**New comprehensive law**: **Law No. 2024/017 of December 23, 2024** -- Cameroon's first standalone personal data protection law. Key features:
- Modeled on GDPR principles: data portability rights, mandatory impact assessments, record-keeping obligations
- Creates a **Personal Data Protection Authority** as supervisory body
- **Compliance deadline: June 23, 2026** -- companies must align data processing protocols by this date
- Covers collection, processing, storage, and transfer of personal data

**Implication for data acquisition**: Any field data collection involving personal information (names, phone numbers, GPS locations tied to individuals) must be designed with this law in mind. The June 2026 deadline is imminent.

Sources: [Kinsmen Advocates 2025 Guide](https://kinsmenadvocates.com/cybersecurity/data-protection-in-cameroon-2025/), [Lex Africa Data Protection Compliance](https://lexafrica.com/2025/10/cameroon-data-protection-law-compliance/), [Halle Law Firm overview](https://www.hallelaw.com/the-protection-of-personal-data-in-cameroon-what-businesses-need-to-know/)

### Existing Geospatial and Mapping Initiatives

- **OpenStreetMap**: Active WikiProject Cameroon exists. Douala has reasonable coverage of major roads but is missing roads on eastern and southeastern outskirts. Building footprints are incomplete. HOT (Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team) has contributed, and local groups like **Eureka Geo** train YouthMappers chapters in Douala. OSM data downloadable from [Geofabrik](https://download.geofabrik.de/africa/cameroon.html). HDX provides [Cameroon Roads exports](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/hotosm_cmr_roads).
- **Google Maps**: Basic coverage exists for Douala but many businesses, especially informal ones, are missing or have inaccurate locations. Street View is absent for most of Douala.
- **GeoCameroun**: A national geospatial initiative referenced on OSM Wiki ([GeoCameroun](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/GeoCameroun)).
- **Fugro** has built a national geodetic network for Cameroon, providing the framework for cadastral mapping.
- **Satellite imagery**: FAO has used AI + satellite imagery for cocoa mapping. Cameroon joined a Congo Basin SPOT satellite initiative for forest monitoring. Urban-focused satellite analytics remain underdeveloped.

### Local Competitors and Similar Data Efforts

- **SiQueries** (Cameroon-based): SaaS data analysis/visualization startup, focused on business intelligence.
- **HData Systems** (Cameroon): Big data analytics and BI services.
- **Amini** (Pan-African): Climate tech using AI and satellite data for environmental data scarcity -- not urban-focused.
- **Cameroon Data Community**: Active LinkedIn group working on land use/land cover mapping using computer vision.
- **Rentechdigital.com SmartScraper**: Provides scraped business listings for Cameroon (gas stations, pharmacies, etc.) -- a potential data source but unverified quality.
- **Tracxn**: Lists 139 GIS companies across Africa, but very few are Cameroon-specific.

**Key gap**: There is no comprehensive, multi-vertical, geolocated data platform focused on Cameroon's urban commercial landscape. This is the core opportunity.

---

## VERTICAL 1: BILLBOARDS

### 1.1 Existing Data Sources

- **Alliance Media** ([alliancemedia.com/cameroon](https://www.alliancemedia.com/cameroon/)): Major outdoor advertising company with billboard inventory in Douala and Yaounde. They likely maintain internal databases of locations.
- **TTNet (Tombih Telecommunications Network)** ([ttnetltd.com](https://www.ttnetltd.com/)): Offers traditional and digital billboard services in Cameroon.
- **Global OOH** ([globalooh.net/market/cameroon](https://globalooh.net/market/cameroon/)): Maintains billboard holdings in Douala and Yaounde.
- **LEDScreenAds.com** ([ledscreenads.com/africa/cameroon](https://www.ledscreenads.com/africa/cameroon)): Lists digital OOH inventory.
- **Super U Bonamoussadi advertising network**: Digital screens at Super U supermarket in Bonamoussadi specifically, noted as a "popular advertising platform in a major commercial hub."
- **Douala Grand Mall**: 6 double-sided screens (Standie Screen Network), 12 screen faces.
- **MINCOM** (Ministry of Communication): Authorizes advertising agencies and issues permits.
- **CUD/Mairie**: Grants physical advertising space in urban areas.

### 1.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

Bonamoussadi sits along major commercial corridors with growing density of billboards, both formal (steel-frame structures by licensed agencies) and informal (hand-painted signs, banners on fences). The ending of the government monopoly previously held by **Cameroon Public-Expansion** has opened the market to multiple operators. Billboards dominate highways, city squares, and commercial districts. In Cameroon, billboards "symbolize credibility" -- a brand's investment in physical space signals stability.

The informal segment is significant: small businesses erect unauthorized signage. Municipalities periodically crack down -- Yaounde municipality, for instance, recently demanded fees for unauthorized ads ([Business in Cameroon](https://www.businessincameroon.com/public-management/1804-13749-yaounde-municipality-cracks-down-on-unauthorized-ads-demands-fees)). Douala likely follows similar patterns.

### 1.3 Update Frequency

- Billboard creative content rotates on varying schedules: major brands (telecom, beer) may run campaigns for 1-6 months; local advertisers change more frequently.
- Physical billboard structures change less often -- new installations or removals happen quarterly to annually.
- Digital screens (Grand Mall, Super U) can change content daily or even hourly.

### 1.4 Data Quality Risks

- **No centralized public registry** of billboard locations or content.
- Many billboards are unauthorized, meaning any municipal database will be incomplete.
- GPS accuracy in dense commercial streets is adequate (5-10m) but billboard facing direction and size require manual verification.
- Content changes are not systematically tracked.

### 1.5 Key Local Actors

- **MINCOM** (Ministry of Communication): Permits
- **CUD / Mairie de Douala V**: Space authorization, taxation
- **Alliance Media, TTNet, Global OOH**: Major operators
- **Local advertising agencies**: Listed at [businesslist.co.cm](https://www.businesslist.co.cm/category/advertising-agencies/city:douala) (87+ agencies nationwide)
- **Cameroon Public-Expansion**: Legacy government agency, diminished role

### 1.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **No georeferenced inventory** of billboard locations exists publicly. Neither municipal nor private-sector databases are open.
- **Content tracking** is nonexistent -- no one systematically records what is displayed on billboards over time.
- **Biggest opportunity**: A comprehensive, geolocated, time-stamped billboard inventory covering Bonamoussadi and greater Douala, capturing location, size, type (static/digital), advertiser, and content. This would be valuable to ad agencies, brands, and the municipality for tax enforcement.

---

## VERTICAL 2: ALCOHOL

### 2.1 Existing Data Sources

- **Euromonitor International**: [Beer in Cameroon](https://www.euromonitor.com/beer-in-cameroon/report), [Alcoholic Drinks in Cameroon](https://www.euromonitor.com/alcoholic-drinks-in-cameroon/report) -- paywalled market research reports.
- **Statista**: [Beer market forecast for Cameroon](https://www.statista.com/outlook/cmo/alcoholic-drinks/beer/cameroon), [Alcoholic Drinks forecast](https://www.statista.com/outlook/cmo/alcoholic-drinks/cameroon).
- **World Bank**: [Total alcohol consumption per capita](https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SH.ALC.PCAP.LI?locations=CM).
- **SABC (Societe Anonyme des Brasseries du Cameroun)**: Internal data on 40,000+ bars and points of sale nationwide. HQ and main factory in Douala.
- **Guinness Cameroon (Diageo)**: ~14.4% market share, has own distribution data.
- **UCB (Union Camerounaise de Brasseries)**: ~7.2% market share.
- **WM Strategy**: [Cameroon Beer Market report](https://www.wm-strategy.com/cameroon-beer-market-market-analysis-size-trends-consumption-insights-opportunities-challenges-and-forecast-until-2024).

### 2.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

Bonamoussadi is one of Douala's major nightlife zones alongside Akwa and Bonapriso. The neighborhood features a dense mix of:
- **Formal establishments**: Licensed bars, nightclubs, restaurants with alcohol licenses
- **Informal drinking spots ("off-licenses")**: Small shops, makeshift bars, and street-side vendors selling beer, palm wine, and locally distilled spirits
- Cameroonians consume over **1 billion bottles of beer per year**. SABC controls 80%+ of the beer market.
- **Distribution**: Beer companies have extensive direct-to-retail distribution networks. Delivery trucks are a common sight.

Informal alcohol production and sale (palm wine, "arki"/local spirits) is substantial but entirely undocumented in formal datasets.

### 2.3 Update Frequency

- Bar/restaurant openings and closures: Monthly churn, especially for informal establishments.
- Alcohol pricing: Regulated at wholesale level but retail prices can vary. Excise tax increases caused major disruption in 2024.
- Product availability: Seasonal (holidays see spikes), and supply chain disruptions from power outages affect availability.

### 2.4 Data Quality Risks

- **Massive informal segment**: Many drinking spots are unregistered. SABC's 40,000-point-of-sale figure likely undercounts.
- **Regulatory sensitivity**: Alcohol advertising is regulated (Law No. 2006/018 on Advertising restricts alcohol promotion).
- **Seasonal variation**: Ramadan (in Muslim-majority northern parts) and holiday periods create fluctuations, though Douala is predominantly Christian and less affected.
- **Health data sensitivity**: Alcohol consumption data can be politically charged.

### 2.5 Key Local Actors

- **SABC (Brasseries du Cameroun)**: Market leader, HQ in Douala, controls distribution data
- **Guinness Cameroon / Diageo**
- **MINCOMMERCE**: Commerce regulation
- **CUD/Mairie**: Business licensing
- **INS Cameroun**: National consumption surveys

### 2.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **No public geospatial registry** of alcohol retail points in Bonamoussadi or Douala.
- **Informal economy blind spot**: Palm wine sellers, unlicensed bars, and street vendors are entirely unmapped.
- **Biggest opportunity**: Geolocated map of all alcohol retail/consumption points, segmented by type (bar, restaurant, off-license, informal), with data on brands available. This would be enormously valuable to SABC, Guinness, and new market entrants like craft breweries.

---

## VERTICAL 3: CENSUS / POPULATION DATA

### 3.1 Existing Data Sources

- **INS Cameroun** (Institut National de la Statistique): [ins-cameroun.cm](https://ins-cameroun.cm/en/). The primary source for census data. Last full census (RGPH) was conducted in **2005**. The 3eme RGPH produced regional projections.
- **Cameroon Data Portal**: [cameroon.opendataforafrica.org](https://cameroon.opendataforafrica.org/) -- Open data under CC-BY 4.0 license, includes population indicators, microdata, and technical documents.
- **NSO Open Data**: [nso-cameroon.opendataforafrica.org](https://nso-cameroon.opendataforafrica.org/) -- Additional open data portal from INS.
- **BUCREP** (Bureau Central des Recensements et des Etudes de Population): Responsible for census operations.
- **U.S. Census Bureau International Database**: Produces arrondissement-level age/sex estimates for Cameroon, extracted 2024. [Technical document](https://www2.census.gov/programs-surveys/international-programs/mapping-files/time-series/Cameroon-Technical-Document.pdf).
- **UNFPA**: [Population data for Cameroon](https://www.unfpa.org/data/world-population/CM).
- **UN World Urbanization Prospects**: Source for Douala metro population estimates.
- **WHO**: [Cameroon health/population data](https://data.who.int/countries/120).

### 3.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

- **The last census is over 20 years old** (2005). All current neighborhood-level data is based on projections, not actual enumeration.
- Bonamoussadi-specific population data simply does not exist in any publicly accessible source. The finest granularity available is arrondissement-level (Douala V).
- Rapid urbanization means 2005-based projections are likely significantly inaccurate for fast-growing neighborhoods like Bonamoussadi.
- Internal migration (rural-to-urban, from anglophone regions due to conflict) has reshaped Douala's demographics since 2005 in ways that projections cannot capture.

### 3.3 Update Frequency

- Full census: Last conducted 2005. A new RGPH has been discussed for years but has not been executed as of early 2026.
- INS surveys (DHS, MICS, household surveys): Conducted irregularly, roughly every 3-5 years.
- Projections: Updated periodically by INS and international organizations, but based on old baseline data.

### 3.4 Data Quality Risks

- **Extreme staleness**: The 2005 baseline is the fundamental problem. Any neighborhood-level estimates are speculative.
- **Political sensitivity**: Census operations in Cameroon are politically charged due to ethnic and regional representation concerns.
- **Undercounting**: Informal settlements, transient populations, and internal refugees are systematically undercounted.
- **No address system**: Bonamoussadi, like most of Douala, lacks a formal street addressing system, making enumeration harder.

### 3.5 Key Local Actors

- **INS Cameroun**: Statistical authority
- **BUCREP**: Census operations
- **CUD**: Local urban data (Plan Directeur d'Urbanisme)
- **UNFPA, World Bank, USAID**: International organizations that fund and support census/survey operations

### 3.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **The entire neighborhood-level population dataset is a gap**. No one has reliable population counts for Bonamoussadi.
- **Opportunity**: Proxy estimation using mobile data (with telecom partnerships), building footprint counts from satellite imagery, or systematic household surveys could produce the first modern population estimate for Bonamoussadi. This would be valuable to government planners, health services, and commercial investors.

---

## VERTICAL 4: FUEL STATIONS

### 4.1 Existing Data Sources

- **CSPH** (Caisse de Stabilisation des Prix des Hydrocarbures): [csph.cm](https://www.csph.cm/) -- Publishes official fuel price structures monthly (e.g., [April 2025 PDF](https://www.csph.cm/dashboard/assets/uploads/pdfs/AVRIL_2025.pdf)).
- **GlobalPetrolPrices.com**: [Cameroon gasoline prices](https://www.globalpetrolprices.com/Cameroon/gasoline_prices/), [diesel prices](https://www.globalpetrolprices.com/Cameroon/diesel_prices/).
- **Ministry of Water and Energy (MINEE)**: Maintains statistics on filling stations by region. Reported **706 filling stations** nationally across 33 distribution companies (as of 2015).
- **Logistics Cluster**: [Cameroon Fuel Assessment](https://lca.logcluster.org/31-cameroon-fuel) -- includes depot locations, pricing structures, and distribution chain.
- **RentechDigital**: Lists **213 gas stations in Douala** as of January 2025 ([source](https://rentechdigital.com/smartscraper/business-report-details/cameroon/littoral/list-of-gas-stations-in-douala)).
- **OpenStreetMap**: Some fuel stations are mapped but coverage is incomplete.

### 4.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

- Major fuel brands present in Douala: **Total Energies** (183 stations nationally, 26% market share), **MRS** (124 stations), **OLA Energy** (formerly Oilibya, 60 stations), **Tradex** (90 stations and growing, state-owned, adding ~5 new stations/year -- [Business in Cameroon](https://www.businessincameroon.com/public-management/2109-15055-tradex-sa-reports-269m-liters-of-fuel-sold-in-2024-expands-into-local-food-at-stations)).
- The Littoral region (which includes Douala) has **175 filling stations** according to MINEE.
- Bonamoussadi, as a major commercial corridor, has several stations from Total, Tradex, and MRS along its main arteries.
- **Informal fuel sales** (from jerry cans on roadsides) also exist, especially in peripheral areas, and are entirely undocumented.
- Current official prices: **Super: 840 FCFA/liter, Gasoil: 828 FCFA/liter** (regulated, uniform nationwide at SCDP depot locations).

### 4.3 Update Frequency

- **Fuel prices**: Regulated by CSPH, adjusted periodically (not on a fixed schedule). Prices were raised in 2023 and have remained relatively stable since.
- **Station openings/closures**: Tradex adds ~5 stations/year; other brands expand more slowly. Net change is gradual.
- **Operational status**: Stations can temporarily close for supply shortages, maintenance, or (during rainy season) flooding. This is dynamic, day-to-day data.

### 4.4 Data Quality Risks

- **Informal fuel sales** are completely off the radar.
- **Station operational status** (open/closed, fuel type availability) is not tracked in real-time by any public source.
- **GPS locations** for stations are generally good (stations are visible, permanent structures) but some OSM/Google Maps entries may be outdated.
- **Supply disruptions**: Power outages and logistics bottlenecks can cause temporary unavailability.

### 4.5 Key Local Actors

- **CSPH**: Price regulation, [csph.cm](https://www.csph.cm/)
- **MINEE**: Ministry of Water and Energy, station licensing
- **SCDP** (Societe Camerounaise des Depots Petroliers): Manages fuel depots including in Douala
- **Total Energies, MRS, OLA Energy, Tradex**: Major distributors
- **SONARA** (National refinery -- though currently non-operational after 2019 fire)

### 4.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **No real-time fuel availability data** exists. Consumers do not know which stations have fuel, which are open, or what fuel types are available.
- **Informal fuel market** is entirely unmapped.
- **Biggest opportunity**: A real-time or near-real-time geolocated fuel station layer showing location, brand, fuel types available, and operational status. This is high-value for consumers (especially during shortages) and for fuel companies tracking distribution effectiveness.

---

## VERTICAL 5: MOBILE MONEY

### 5.1 Existing Data Sources

- **ART (Agence de Regulation des Telecommunications)**: [art.cm](https://www.art.cm/en) -- Annual market observatory report. 2024 data: 11 million+ active mobile money subscribers, revenues up 146%.
- **MTN MoMo**: CFA 70.2 billion revenue in 2024 (up 27% from 2023).
- **Orange Money**: CFA 65 billion revenue in 2024. 11 million+ customers in Cameroon.
- **GSMA Mobile Money reports**: Pan-African mobile money data.
- **Market projections**: Cameroon Mobile Money market at USD 40.8 million, projected to reach USD 154.7 million by 2031 (24.86% CAGR).
- **Business in Cameroon**: Regular reporting on mobile money trends ([source](https://www.businessincameroon.com/finance/1012-15480-cameroon-s-mobile-money-industry-accelerates-topping-cfa135-84bn)).

### 5.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

- Mobile money kiosks/agents are ubiquitous in Bonamoussadi. Nearly every commercial street has multiple MTN MoMo and Orange Money agent points.
- **Agent types**: Branded kiosks (yellow for MTN, orange for Orange), small shops with agent signage, and individual agents operating from tables or market stalls.
- **New entrants**: **Wave** (US fintech, low-fee model) is disrupting the duopoly. **CAMTEL plans to launch Blue Money in 2026**.
- Mobile money is used for: P2P transfers, bill payments, merchant payments, salary disbursement, savings products.
- **Agent liquidity** is a real operational constraint -- agents in high-traffic areas like Bonamoussadi can run out of float during peak periods.

### 5.3 Update Frequency

- Agent locations: Moderate churn. New agents open frequently; some close or become inactive within months. Monthly monitoring is appropriate.
- Transaction volumes: Vary daily and hourly. ART publishes annual data; operators have real-time internal data.
- New service launches (Wave, Blue Money): Quarterly impact on competitive landscape.

### 5.4 Data Quality Risks

- **Agent registration vs. activity**: An agent may be registered but inactive. Telecom operators have this data but do not share it publicly.
- **Multi-brand agents**: Some agents serve both MTN and Orange, complicating categorization.
- **Location precision**: Agents are often in small, unmarked locations that are hard to geolocate precisely.
- **Regulatory data**: ART data is aggregated nationally, not available at city or neighborhood level.

### 5.5 Key Local Actors

- **ART Cameroon**: Telecom regulator, [art.cm](https://www.art.cm/en)
- **MTN Cameroon / MTN MoMo**
- **Orange Cameroon / Orange Money**
- **CAMTEL** (state-owned, launching Blue Money)
- **Wave** (new entrant)
- **BEAC** (Bank of Central African States): Regional mobile money regulation

### 5.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **No public map of mobile money agent locations** exists. Operators maintain internal registries but do not share them.
- **Agent liquidity data** is not available -- a major pain point for users.
- **Biggest opportunity**: Geolocated map of all mobile money agent points in Bonamoussadi, with brand, status (active/inactive), and potentially crowdsourced liquidity information. This is valuable to operators (for network optimization), regulators (for financial inclusion metrics), and consumers.

---

## VERTICAL 6: PHARMACY

### 6.1 Existing Data Sources

- **DPML** (Direction de la Pharmacie, du Medicament et des Laboratoires): [dpml.cm](https://dpml.cm/) / [dpml.agisoin.com](https://dpml.agisoin.com/index.php/en/) -- Maintains the official marketing authorization (AMM) registry and lists of licensed pharmaceutical establishments, including responsible pharmacist names and locations.
- **CNOPC** (Conseil National de l'Ordre des Pharmaciens du Cameroun): [ordrepharmacien.cm](https://ordrepharmacien.cm/) -- Professional body that registers pharmacists.
- **businesslist.co.cm**: Lists [19+ pharmacies in Douala](https://www.businesslist.co.cm/category/pharmacies/city:douala) (likely incomplete).
- **RentechDigital SmartScraper**: Reports **512 pharmacies in Cameroon** total, with **197 in Littoral region** (which includes Douala). Douala city has **300+ pharmacies**.
- **Pharmacies Cameroun mobile app**: Available on [Google Play](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.touss.wilson.pharmaciescameroun), covers pharmacies in 40+ towns.
- **pharmaciedeshopitaux.com**: [Pharmacy directory](https://pharmaciedeshopitaux.com/index.php/annuaire).

### 6.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

- Bonamoussadi has a relatively high density of pharmacies given its middle-class population.
- **Formal pharmacies**: Licensed, registered with DPML and CNOPC, required to have a qualified pharmacist on-site.
- **Informal drug sellers ("pharmacies de rue")**: Unlicensed vendors selling medications in markets and on streets. This is illegal but widespread.
- **Night pharmacies (pharmacies de garde)**: A rotating duty system exists where certain pharmacies stay open overnight and on holidays. This rotation is published but not always reliably.
- **Opening a pharmacy requires**: DPML authorization, CNOPC registration, MINCOMMERCE business registration, and municipal permits. Requirements documented at [primetimelawoffice.com](https://primetimelawoffice.com/requirements-to-open-a-pharmacy-in-cameroon/).

### 6.3 Update Frequency

- Pharmacy openings/closures: Quarterly to annual churn. Opening a pharmacy involves lengthy regulatory process (months).
- Pharmacie de garde rotations: Weekly or bi-weekly schedules.
- Drug availability: Can change daily due to supply chain issues and import dependencies.
- Inspections: Required at least once annually by law.

### 6.4 Data Quality Risks

- **DPML records may lag reality**: Pharmacies can close or relocate without timely updates to official records.
- **Informal drug sellers** are entirely absent from official databases.
- **Location data**: Official records often use descriptive addresses ("near the roundabout"), not GPS coordinates.
- **Medication availability**: No public real-time tracking of drug stock levels.

### 6.5 Key Local Actors

- **DPML / MINSANTE**: Pharmaceutical regulation
- **CNOPC**: Professional pharmacist body
- **CENAME** (Centrale Nationale d'Approvisionnement en Medicaments): National drug supply
- **LANACOME**: Drug quality control laboratory
- **Private distributors**: Listed in [DPML directory](https://dpml.cm/images/Repertoire/EP/Societes_Distribution_Produits_Pharmaceutiques_23_09_2021.pdf)
- **Maligah.com**: Online pharmacy directory platform

### 6.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **No geolocated pharmacy map with real-time data** (opening hours, de-garde status, drug availability).
- **Informal drug market** is unmapped and a public health concern.
- **Biggest opportunity**: A comprehensive pharmacy layer with GPS coordinates, opening hours, pharmacie-de-garde rotation, and (ideally) drug availability indicators. Public health value is high; commercial value exists for pharmaceutical distributors.

---

## VERTICAL 7: TRANSPORT / ROADS (INCLUDING BLOCKED ROADS)

### 7.1 Existing Data Sources

- **OpenStreetMap**: [Cameroon Roads on HDX](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/hotosm_cmr_roads) -- exportable road network, though incomplete for eastern/southeastern Douala.
- **JICA**: Conducted a [Data Collection Survey on Transport Network Development](https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/12286837_01.pdf) for Cameroon.
- **MobiliseYourCity**: [Support program in Cameroon](https://www.mobiliseyourcity.net/mobiliseyourcitys-support-cameroon) -- urban mobility planning.
- **CUD Plan Directeur d'Urbanisme**: [Master Urban Plan](https://www.fsmtoolbox.com/assets/pdf/Plan_Directeur_d_urbanisme.pdf) -- road network planning document.
- **Yango**: Active ride-hailing service in Douala ([yango.com/en_cm/city/douala](https://yango.com/en_cm/city/douala/)), collects GPS trace data. Uses AI routing that adapts to real-time traffic.
- **Academic research**: GPS-based study of motorcycle taxi activity in Cameroon published in Journal of Transport Geography ([ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0966692318309141)).
- **Cameroon-streets.openalfa.com**: Street-level guides for [Bonamoussadi](https://cameroon-streets.openalfa.com/bonamoussadi) and sub-neighborhoods.

### 7.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

- **Road conditions**: Douala's roads suffer rapid degradation. Only ~10% of Cameroon's roadways are tarred. Even newly constructed roads develop potholes quickly due to poor construction quality. [Cameroon Tribune](https://www.cameroon-tribune.cm/article.html/33809/fr.html/rapid-degradation-of-roads-in-douala).
- **Seasonal flooding**: Douala IV and V (which includes Bonamoussadi) are particularly vulnerable. Floods in June-September block roads, damage infrastructure, and cut off areas. [Flood-prone area maps exist in academic literature](https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Map-of-flood-prone-areas-in-Douala_fig2_273321869).
- **Transport modes**: Moto-taxis ("bendskin") account for **40% of trips**. Estimated 22,000-25,000 moto-taxis in Douala. SOCATUR buses handle less than 1% of trips. Personal vehicles, shared taxis, and Yango ride-hailing fill the rest.
- **Road blocks**: Caused by flooding (seasonal), construction works, accidents, and occasionally political demonstrations. No systematic reporting mechanism exists.
- **Emergency road plan**: Mayor Roger Mbassa Ndine announced emergency road rehabilitation in August 2024 after heavy rain damage.

### 7.3 Update Frequency

- **Road conditions**: Change with seasons. Rainy season (June-September) causes rapid deterioration. Post-rainy season repairs occur gradually.
- **Blocked roads**: Can change hourly during rainy season. Construction blockages last days to months.
- **New road construction**: Major projects (like Yaounde-Douala Highway Phase II, 880 billion FCFA, target 2028) are multi-year.
- **Traffic patterns**: Daily variation. No public real-time traffic data exists aside from Yango/Google Maps routing.

### 7.4 Data Quality Risks

- **OSM road data is static** and not updated for seasonal flooding or construction.
- **No systematic blocked-road reporting** -- information travels by word of mouth and social media.
- **GPS traces from ride-hailing** are private (Yango does not share this data).
- **Informal routes** (unpaved shortcuts through neighborhoods) exist but are not mapped.
- **Address system absence**: Without formal addresses, navigation relies on landmarks, making routing data less structured.

### 7.5 Key Local Actors

- **CUD / Mairie de Douala**: Road maintenance, urban planning
- **MINTP** (Ministry of Public Works): National road infrastructure
- **MINT** (Ministry of Transport): Transport regulation
- **Yango**: Ride-hailing, GPS data collection
- **SOCATUR**: Bus operator
- **Moto-taxi unions/syndicats**: Informal but organized groups with ground knowledge
- **World Bank**: Funds urban transport and infrastructure projects

### 7.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **No real-time or near-real-time road condition / blocked road data** exists publicly.
- **Flood-risk overlay** on road maps does not exist in an operational, consumer-facing format.
- **Moto-taxi route patterns** are unstudied outside of a few academic papers.
- **Biggest opportunity**: A dynamic road condition layer that combines static road network data (from OSM) with crowdsourced or sensor-based updates on road blockages, flooding, and construction. During rainy season, this is life-or-death information for commuters. Value extends to logistics companies, emergency services, and urban planners.

---

## SUMMARY OF GAPS AND STRATEGIC OPPORTUNITIES

| Vertical | Key Gap | Opportunity Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Billboards** | No georeferenced inventory; no content tracking | High (advertising industry, municipal tax enforcement) |
| **Alcohol** | Informal drinking spots unmapped; no retail point registry | High (SABC, Guinness, new entrants) |
| **Census** | Last census 2005; no neighborhood-level data | Very High (government, health, commercial planning) |
| **Fuel** | No real-time station status or availability data | High (consumers, fuel companies) |
| **Mobile Money** | No public agent location map; no liquidity data | Very High (operators, regulators, consumers) |
| **Pharmacy** | No geolocated directory with hours/garde status | High (public health, pharmaceutical distributors) |
| **Transport/Roads** | No real-time road condition or blocked-road data | Very High (commuters, logistics, emergency services) |

**Cross-cutting insight**: The fundamental enabler across all verticals is **geolocated, regularly updated field data** -- something that neither government agencies nor the private sector currently provide for Bonamoussadi or Douala at the required granularity. The combination of high mobile penetration (96.4%), growing smartphone adoption, and the absence of any comprehensive urban data platform creates a clear market opening.

**Regulatory timeline note**: The June 23, 2026 compliance deadline for Law No. 2024/017 (data protection) means any data collection methodology must be designed with privacy-by-design principles from the start.

---

Sources:
- [Douala Population - MacroTrends](https://www.macrotrends.net/global-metrics/cities/20362/douala/population)
- [Alliance Media Cameroon](https://www.alliancemedia.com/cameroon/)
- [TTNet Billboard Services](https://www.ttnetltd.com/combining-traditional-digital-marketing-billboards-social-media-in-cameroon/)

You are Teammate 1 — the Data Analyst — in a 9-agent team building a data acquisition strategy for Bonamoussadi, Douala, Cameroon.

Your job: Research the local context for these 7 verticals and produce a structured report. Use web search extensively.

VERTICALS: billboards, alcohol, census, fuel, mobile money, pharmacy, transport roads (including blocked roads)

For EACH vertical, research and document:

1. **Existing data sources** — What structured datasets exist today? (government, NGO, private sector, open data portals, OSM, satellite imagery providers, telecom data, etc.)
2. **Field realities in Bonamoussadi/Douala** — What does the ground truth look like? Density of businesses, informal vs formal economy split, local languages, accessibility, infrastructure quality.
3. **Update frequency** — How often does this data change? (billboards rotate monthly? fuel prices change weekly? pharmacies open/close quarterly?)
4. **Data quality risks** — What are the main risks: outdated records, informal businesses not registered, GPS accuracy issues in dense urban areas, seasonal variations, political sensitivities (census), etc.
5. **Key local actors** — Who collects, owns, or brokers this data locally? (Mairie de Douala, INS Cameroun, MINCOMMERCE, telecom operators like MTN/Orange, local advertising agencies, etc.)
6. **Gaps and opportunities** — What data is simply not collected today? Where is the biggest opportunity for a new data layer?

Also research:
- Cameroon's data protection law (Loi n°2010/012) and any recent updates
- Mobile penetration rates and smartphone adoption in Douala
- Internet connectivity quality in Bonamoussadi
- Existing geospatial/mapping initiatives (OSM coverage, Google Maps completeness, what's missing)
- Local competitors or similar data collection efforts

Format your output as a clean, comprehensive report with clear sections per vertical plus a cross-cutting section on infrastructure and regulatory context. Be specific — cite actual organizations, laws, datasets, and URLs where possible.

OUTPUT THIS AS A COMPLETE REPORT — do not write files. Just return the full text.

content.text:
Now I have enough data to compile the comprehensive report. Here it is:

---

# DATA ACQUISITION STRATEGY: BONAMOUSSADI, DOUALA, CAMEROON
## Local Context Research Report -- 7 Verticals

---

## CROSS-CUTTING CONTEXT: INFRASTRUCTURE, REGULATORY, AND DIGITAL LANDSCAPE

### Geographic and Demographic Context

Bonamoussadi is located within the **Douala V** (Douala 5eme) arrondissement of Douala, Cameroon's economic capital. Douala's metro population reached approximately **4.35 million** in 2025 (a 3.4% annual increase). The city is divided into six communes d'arrondissement, managed by the **Communaute Urbaine de Douala (CUD)**, which covers 923 km2 with 21,000 hectares allocated to housing, commercial, and industrial uses.

Bonamoussadi was historically a residential neighborhood but has experienced rapid commercialization, with malls, supermarkets, restaurants, and nightclubs proliferating in recent years. It is one of Douala's more middle-class and upper-middle-class areas, making it both denser and more formalized than many other Douala neighborhoods -- though informality remains pervasive.

**Language**: Douala is primarily francophone. In 2014, 76.4% of Douala inhabitants over 15 spoke and understood French. English is also an official language but secondary here. Local languages (Duala, Bassa, Bamileke dialects) are common in daily commerce.

### Mobile Penetration and Smartphone Adoption

- Mobile connections in Cameroon reached **96.4% of total population** (October 2025), with **31.5 million active subscriptions** -- up 15.8% in 2024.
- **87.5%** of mobile connections are broadband (3G/4G/5G).
- **12.6 million** internet users (41.9% penetration) as of end 2025.
- Sub-Saharan Africa smartphone adoption stood at ~55% in 2023; Cameroon is likely in the 45-55% range.
- Market share: **Orange Cameroon 50.08%**, **MTN Cameroon 47.2%**, **CAMTEL 2.67%**.

Sources: [DataReportal Digital 2026: Cameroon](https://datareportal.com/reports/digital-2026-cameroon), [TechAfrica News on telecom growth](https://techafricanews.com/2025/12/09/cameroons-telecom-sector-grows-18-in-2024-driven-by-mobile-and-digital-services/)

### Internet Connectivity in Bonamoussadi/Douala

- Orange and MTN provide 3G/4G coverage across Douala. Coverage maps available at [nPerf.com](https://www.nperf.com/en/map/CM/2232593.Douala/-./signal).
- CAMTEL provides fixed broadband via fiber optic in select areas. CAMTEL coverage map: [camtel.cm/coverage](https://camtel.cm/coverage).
- A DOUALA-IX internet exchange point exists, enhancing regional connectivity ([douala-ix.net](https://www.douala-ix.net/)).
- 5G deployment is beginning at the Port of Douala-Bonaberi, but consumer 5G is not yet widespread.
- **Reality check**: In dense urban neighborhoods like Bonamoussadi, 4G speeds can be inconsistent due to congestion, especially during peak hours. Power outages (frequent in Cameroon) further disrupt connectivity.

### Cameroon Data Protection Law

**Old regime**: Law No. 2010/012 of December 21, 2010 on Cybersecurity and Cybercrime, which included provisions on privacy and data retention.

**New comprehensive law**: **Law No. 2024/017 of December 23, 2024** -- Cameroon's first standalone personal data protection law. Key features:
- Modeled on GDPR principles: data portability rights, mandatory impact assessments, record-keeping obligations
- Creates a **Personal Data Protection Authority** as supervisory body
- **Compliance deadline: June 23, 2026** -- companies must align data processing protocols by this date
- Covers collection, processing, storage, and transfer of personal data

**Implication for data acquisition**: Any field data collection involving personal information (names, phone numbers, GPS locations tied to individuals) must be designed with this law in mind. The June 2026 deadline is imminent.

Sources: [Kinsmen Advocates 2025 Guide](https://kinsmenadvocates.com/cybersecurity/data-protection-in-cameroon-2025/), [Lex Africa Data Protection Compliance](https://lexafrica.com/2025/10/cameroon-data-protection-law-compliance/), [Halle Law Firm overview](https://www.hallelaw.com/the-protection-of-personal-data-in-cameroon-what-businesses-need-to-know/)

### Existing Geospatial and Mapping Initiatives

- **OpenStreetMap**: Active WikiProject Cameroon exists. Douala has reasonable coverage of major roads but is missing roads on eastern and southeastern outskirts. Building footprints are incomplete. HOT (Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team) has contributed, and local groups like **Eureka Geo** train YouthMappers chapters in Douala. OSM data downloadable from [Geofabrik](https://download.geofabrik.de/africa/cameroon.html). HDX provides [Cameroon Roads exports](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/hotosm_cmr_roads).
- **Google Maps**: Basic coverage exists for Douala but many businesses, especially informal ones, are missing or have inaccurate locations. Street View is absent for most of Douala.
- **GeoCameroun**: A national geospatial initiative referenced on OSM Wiki ([GeoCameroun](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/GeoCameroun)).
- **Fugro** has built a national geodetic network for Cameroon, providing the framework for cadastral mapping.
- **Satellite imagery**: FAO has used AI + satellite imagery for cocoa mapping. Cameroon joined a Congo Basin SPOT satellite initiative for forest monitoring. Urban-focused satellite analytics remain underdeveloped.

### Local Competitors and Similar Data Efforts

- **SiQueries** (Cameroon-based): SaaS data analysis/visualization startup, focused on business intelligence.
- **HData Systems** (Cameroon): Big data analytics and BI services.
- **Amini** (Pan-African): Climate tech using AI and satellite data for environmental data scarcity -- not urban-focused.
- **Cameroon Data Community**: Active LinkedIn group working on land use/land cover mapping using computer vision.
- **Rentechdigital.com SmartScraper**: Provides scraped business listings for Cameroon (gas stations, pharmacies, etc.) -- a potential data source but unverified quality.
- **Tracxn**: Lists 139 GIS companies across Africa, but very few are Cameroon-specific.

**Key gap**: There is no comprehensive, multi-vertical, geolocated data platform focused on Cameroon's urban commercial landscape. This is the core opportunity.

---

## VERTICAL 1: BILLBOARDS

### 1.1 Existing Data Sources

- **Alliance Media** ([alliancemedia.com/cameroon](https://www.alliancemedia.com/cameroon/)): Major outdoor advertising company with billboard inventory in Douala and Yaounde. They likely maintain internal databases of locations.
- **TTNet (Tombih Telecommunications Network)** ([ttnetltd.com](https://www.ttnetltd.com/)): Offers traditional and digital billboard services in Cameroon.
- **Global OOH** ([globalooh.net/market/cameroon](https://globalooh.net/market/cameroon/)): Maintains billboard holdings in Douala and Yaounde.
- **LEDScreenAds.com** ([ledscreenads.com/africa/cameroon](https://www.ledscreenads.com/africa/cameroon)): Lists digital OOH inventory.
- **Super U Bonamoussadi advertising network**: Digital screens at Super U supermarket in Bonamoussadi specifically, noted as a "popular advertising platform in a major commercial hub."
- **Douala Grand Mall**: 6 double-sided screens (Standie Screen Network), 12 screen faces.
- **MINCOM** (Ministry of Communication): Authorizes advertising agencies and issues permits.
- **CUD/Mairie**: Grants physical advertising space in urban areas.

### 1.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

Bonamoussadi sits along major commercial corridors with growing density of billboards, both formal (steel-frame structures by licensed agencies) and informal (hand-painted signs, banners on fences). The ending of the government monopoly previously held by **Cameroon Public-Expansion** has opened the market to multiple operators. Billboards dominate highways, city squares, and commercial districts. In Cameroon, billboards "symbolize credibility" -- a brand's investment in physical space signals stability.

The informal segment is significant: small businesses erect unauthorized signage. Municipalities periodically crack down -- Yaounde municipality, for instance, recently demanded fees for unauthorized ads ([Business in Cameroon](https://www.businessincameroon.com/public-management/1804-13749-yaounde-municipality-cracks-down-on-unauthorized-ads-demands-fees)). Douala likely follows similar patterns.

### 1.3 Update Frequency

- Billboard creative content rotates on varying schedules: major brands (telecom, beer) may run campaigns for 1-6 months; local advertisers change more frequently.
- Physical billboard structures change less often -- new installations or removals happen quarterly to annually.
- Digital screens (Grand Mall, Super U) can change content daily or even hourly.

### 1.4 Data Quality Risks

- **No centralized public registry** of billboard locations or content.
- Many billboards are unauthorized, meaning any municipal database will be incomplete.
- GPS accuracy in dense commercial streets is adequate (5-10m) but billboard facing direction and size require manual verification.
- Content changes are not systematically tracked.

### 1.5 Key Local Actors

- **MINCOM** (Ministry of Communication): Permits
- **CUD / Mairie de Douala V**: Space authorization, taxation
- **Alliance Media, TTNet, Global OOH**: Major operators
- **Local advertising agencies**: Listed at [businesslist.co.cm](https://www.businesslist.co.cm/category/advertising-agencies/city:douala) (87+ agencies nationwide)
- **Cameroon Public-Expansion**: Legacy government agency, diminished role

### 1.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **No georeferenced inventory** of billboard locations exists publicly. Neither municipal nor private-sector databases are open.
- **Content tracking** is nonexistent -- no one systematically records what is displayed on billboards over time.
- **Biggest opportunity**: A comprehensive, geolocated, time-stamped billboard inventory covering Bonamoussadi and greater Douala, capturing location, size, type (static/digital), advertiser, and content. This would be valuable to ad agencies, brands, and the municipality for tax enforcement.

---

## VERTICAL 2: ALCOHOL

### 2.1 Existing Data Sources

- **Euromonitor International**: [Beer in Cameroon](https://www.euromonitor.com/beer-in-cameroon/report), [Alcoholic Drinks in Cameroon](https://www.euromonitor.com/alcoholic-drinks-in-cameroon/report) -- paywalled market research reports.
- **Statista**: [Beer market forecast for Cameroon](https://www.statista.com/outlook/cmo/alcoholic-drinks/beer/cameroon), [Alcoholic Drinks forecast](https://www.statista.com/outlook/cmo/alcoholic-drinks/cameroon).
- **World Bank**: [Total alcohol consumption per capita](https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SH.ALC.PCAP.LI?locations=CM).
- **SABC (Societe Anonyme des Brasseries du Cameroun)**: Internal data on 40,000+ bars and points of sale nationwide. HQ and main factory in Douala.
- **Guinness Cameroon (Diageo)**: ~14.4% market share, has own distribution data.
- **UCB (Union Camerounaise de Brasseries)**: ~7.2% market share.
- **WM Strategy**: [Cameroon Beer Market report](https://www.wm-strategy.com/cameroon-beer-market-market-analysis-size-trends-consumption-insights-opportunities-challenges-and-forecast-until-2024).

### 2.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

Bonamoussadi is one of Douala's major nightlife zones alongside Akwa and Bonapriso. The neighborhood features a dense mix of:
- **Formal establishments**: Licensed bars, nightclubs, restaurants with alcohol licenses
- **Informal drinking spots ("off-licenses")**: Small shops, makeshift bars, and street-side vendors selling beer, palm wine, and locally distilled spirits
- Cameroonians consume over **1 billion bottles of beer per year**. SABC controls 80%+ of the beer market.
- **Distribution**: Beer companies have extensive direct-to-retail distribution networks. Delivery trucks are a common sight.

Informal alcohol production and sale (palm wine, "arki"/local spirits) is substantial but entirely undocumented in formal datasets.

### 2.3 Update Frequency

- Bar/restaurant openings and closures: Monthly churn, especially for informal establishments.
- Alcohol pricing: Regulated at wholesale level but retail prices can vary. Excise tax increases caused major disruption in 2024.
- Product availability: Seasonal (holidays see spikes), and supply chain disruptions from power outages affect availability.

### 2.4 Data Quality Risks

- **Massive informal segment**: Many drinking spots are unregistered. SABC's 40,000-point-of-sale figure likely undercounts.
- **Regulatory sensitivity**: Alcohol advertising is regulated (Law No. 2006/018 on Advertising restricts alcohol promotion).
- **Seasonal variation**: Ramadan (in Muslim-majority northern parts) and holiday periods create fluctuations, though Douala is predominantly Christian and less affected.
- **Health data sensitivity**: Alcohol consumption data can be politically charged.

### 2.5 Key Local Actors

- **SABC (Brasseries du Cameroun)**: Market leader, HQ in Douala, controls distribution data
- **Guinness Cameroon / Diageo**
- **MINCOMMERCE**: Commerce regulation
- **CUD/Mairie**: Business licensing
- **INS Cameroun**: National consumption surveys

### 2.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **No public geospatial registry** of alcohol retail points in Bonamoussadi or Douala.
- **Informal economy blind spot**: Palm wine sellers, unlicensed bars, and street vendors are entirely unmapped.
- **Biggest opportunity**: Geolocated map of all alcohol retail/consumption points, segmented by type (bar, restaurant, off-license, informal), with data on brands available. This would be enormously valuable to SABC, Guinness, and new market entrants like craft breweries.

---

## VERTICAL 3: CENSUS / POPULATION DATA

### 3.1 Existing Data Sources

- **INS Cameroun** (Institut National de la Statistique): [ins-cameroun.cm](https://ins-cameroun.cm/en/). The primary source for census data. Last full census (RGPH) was conducted in **2005**. The 3eme RGPH produced regional projections.
- **Cameroon Data Portal**: [cameroon.opendataforafrica.org](https://cameroon.opendataforafrica.org/) -- Open data under CC-BY 4.0 license, includes population indicators, microdata, and technical documents.
- **NSO Open Data**: [nso-cameroon.opendataforafrica.org](https://nso-cameroon.opendataforafrica.org/) -- Additional open data portal from INS.
- **BUCREP** (Bureau Central des Recensements et des Etudes de Population): Responsible for census operations.
- **U.S. Census Bureau International Database**: Produces arrondissement-level age/sex estimates for Cameroon, extracted 2024. [Technical document](https://www2.census.gov/programs-surveys/international-programs/mapping-files/time-series/Cameroon-Technical-Document.pdf).
- **UNFPA**: [Population data for Cameroon](https://www.unfpa.org/data/world-population/CM).
- **UN World Urbanization Prospects**: Source for Douala metro population estimates.
- **WHO**: [Cameroon health/population data](https://data.who.int/countries/120).

### 3.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

- **The last census is over 20 years old** (2005). All current neighborhood-level data is based on projections, not actual enumeration.
- Bonamoussadi-specific population data simply does not exist in any publicly accessible source. The finest granularity available is arrondissement-level (Douala V).
- Rapid urbanization means 2005-based projections are likely significantly inaccurate for fast-growing neighborhoods like Bonamoussadi.
- Internal migration (rural-to-urban, from anglophone regions due to conflict) has reshaped Douala's demographics since 2005 in ways that projections cannot capture.

### 3.3 Update Frequency

- Full census: Last conducted 2005. A new RGPH has been discussed for years but has not been executed as of early 2026.
- INS surveys (DHS, MICS, household surveys): Conducted irregularly, roughly every 3-5 years.
- Projections: Updated periodically by INS and international organizations, but based on old baseline data.

### 3.4 Data Quality Risks

- **Extreme staleness**: The 2005 baseline is the fundamental problem. Any neighborhood-level estimates are speculative.
- **Political sensitivity**: Census operations in Cameroon are politically charged due to ethnic and regional representation concerns.
- **Undercounting**: Informal settlements, transient populations, and internal refugees are systematically undercounted.
- **No address system**: Bonamoussadi, like most of Douala, lacks a formal street addressing system, making enumeration harder.

### 3.5 Key Local Actors

- **INS Cameroun**: Statistical authority
- **BUCREP**: Census operations
- **CUD**: Local urban data (Plan Directeur d'Urbanisme)
- **UNFPA, World Bank, USAID**: International organizations that fund and support census/survey operations

### 3.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **The entire neighborhood-level population dataset is a gap**. No one has reliable population counts for Bonamoussadi.
- **Opportunity**: Proxy estimation using mobile data (with telecom partnerships), building footprint counts from satellite imagery, or systematic household surveys could produce the first modern population estimate for Bonamoussadi. This would be valuable to government planners, health services, and commercial investors.

---

## VERTICAL 4: FUEL STATIONS

### 4.1 Existing Data Sources

- **CSPH** (Caisse de Stabilisation des Prix des Hydrocarbures): [csph.cm](https://www.csph.cm/) -- Publishes official fuel price structures monthly (e.g., [April 2025 PDF](https://www.csph.cm/dashboard/assets/uploads/pdfs/AVRIL_2025.pdf)).
- **GlobalPetrolPrices.com**: [Cameroon gasoline prices](https://www.globalpetrolprices.com/Cameroon/gasoline_prices/), [diesel prices](https://www.globalpetrolprices.com/Cameroon/diesel_prices/).
- **Ministry of Water and Energy (MINEE)**: Maintains statistics on filling stations by region. Reported **706 filling stations** nationally across 33 distribution companies (as of 2015).
- **Logistics Cluster**: [Cameroon Fuel Assessment](https://lca.logcluster.org/31-cameroon-fuel) -- includes depot locations, pricing structures, and distribution chain.
- **RentechDigital**: Lists **213 gas stations in Douala** as of January 2025 ([source](https://rentechdigital.com/smartscraper/business-report-details/cameroon/littoral/list-of-gas-stations-in-douala)).
- **OpenStreetMap**: Some fuel stations are mapped but coverage is incomplete.

### 4.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

- Major fuel brands present in Douala: **Total Energies** (183 stations nationally, 26% market share), **MRS** (124 stations), **OLA Energy** (formerly Oilibya, 60 stations), **Tradex** (90 stations and growing, state-owned, adding ~5 new stations/year -- [Business in Cameroon](https://www.businessincameroon.com/public-management/2109-15055-tradex-sa-reports-269m-liters-of-fuel-sold-in-2024-expands-into-local-food-at-stations)).
- The Littoral region (which includes Douala) has **175 filling stations** according to MINEE.
- Bonamoussadi, as a major commercial corridor, has several stations from Total, Tradex, and MRS along its main arteries.
- **Informal fuel sales** (from jerry cans on roadsides) also exist, especially in peripheral areas, and are entirely undocumented.
- Current official prices: **Super: 840 FCFA/liter, Gasoil: 828 FCFA/liter** (regulated, uniform nationwide at SCDP depot locations).

### 4.3 Update Frequency

- **Fuel prices**: Regulated by CSPH, adjusted periodically (not on a fixed schedule). Prices were raised in 2023 and have remained relatively stable since.
- **Station openings/closures**: Tradex adds ~5 stations/year; other brands expand more slowly. Net change is gradual.
- **Operational status**: Stations can temporarily close for supply shortages, maintenance, or (during rainy season) flooding. This is dynamic, day-to-day data.

### 4.4 Data Quality Risks

- **Informal fuel sales** are completely off the radar.
- **Station operational status** (open/closed, fuel type availability) is not tracked in real-time by any public source.
- **GPS locations** for stations are generally good (stations are visible, permanent structures) but some OSM/Google Maps entries may be outdated.
- **Supply disruptions**: Power outages and logistics bottlenecks can cause temporary unavailability.

### 4.5 Key Local Actors

- **CSPH**: Price regulation, [csph.cm](https://www.csph.cm/)
- **MINEE**: Ministry of Water and Energy, station licensing
- **SCDP** (Societe Camerounaise des Depots Petroliers): Manages fuel depots including in Douala
- **Total Energies, MRS, OLA Energy, Tradex**: Major distributors
- **SONARA** (National refinery -- though currently non-operational after 2019 fire)

### 4.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **No real-time fuel availability data** exists. Consumers do not know which stations have fuel, which are open, or what fuel types are available.
- **Informal fuel market** is entirely unmapped.
- **Biggest opportunity**: A real-time or near-real-time geolocated fuel station layer showing location, brand, fuel types available, and operational status. This is high-value for consumers (especially during shortages) and for fuel companies tracking distribution effectiveness.

---

## VERTICAL 5: MOBILE MONEY

### 5.1 Existing Data Sources

- **ART (Agence de Regulation des Telecommunications)**: [art.cm](https://www.art.cm/en) -- Annual market observatory report. 2024 data: 11 million+ active mobile money subscribers, revenues up 146%.
- **MTN MoMo**: CFA 70.2 billion revenue in 2024 (up 27% from 2023).
- **Orange Money**: CFA 65 billion revenue in 2024. 11 million+ customers in Cameroon.
- **GSMA Mobile Money reports**: Pan-African mobile money data.
- **Market projections**: Cameroon Mobile Money market at USD 40.8 million, projected to reach USD 154.7 million by 2031 (24.86% CAGR).
- **Business in Cameroon**: Regular reporting on mobile money trends ([source](https://www.businessincameroon.com/finance/1012-15480-cameroon-s-mobile-money-industry-accelerates-topping-cfa135-84bn)).

### 5.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

- Mobile money kiosks/agents are ubiquitous in Bonamoussadi. Nearly every commercial street has multiple MTN MoMo and Orange Money agent points.
- **Agent types**: Branded kiosks (yellow for MTN, orange for Orange), small shops with agent signage, and individual agents operating from tables or market stalls.
- **New entrants**: **Wave** (US fintech, low-fee model) is disrupting the duopoly. **CAMTEL plans to launch Blue Money in 2026**.
- Mobile money is used for: P2P transfers, bill payments, merchant payments, salary disbursement, savings products.
- **Agent liquidity** is a real operational constraint -- agents in high-traffic areas like Bonamoussadi can run out of float during peak periods.

### 5.3 Update Frequency

- Agent locations: Moderate churn. New agents open frequently; some close or become inactive within months. Monthly monitoring is appropriate.
- Transaction volumes: Vary daily and hourly. ART publishes annual data; operators have real-time internal data.
- New service launches (Wave, Blue Money): Quarterly impact on competitive landscape.

### 5.4 Data Quality Risks

- **Agent registration vs. activity**: An agent may be registered but inactive. Telecom operators have this data but do not share it publicly.
- **Multi-brand agents**: Some agents serve both MTN and Orange, complicating categorization.
- **Location precision**: Agents are often in small, unmarked locations that are hard to geolocate precisely.
- **Regulatory data**: ART data is aggregated nationally, not available at city or neighborhood level.

### 5.5 Key Local Actors

- **ART Cameroon**: Telecom regulator, [art.cm](https://www.art.cm/en)
- **MTN Cameroon / MTN MoMo**
- **Orange Cameroon / Orange Money**
- **CAMTEL** (state-owned, launching Blue Money)
- **Wave** (new entrant)
- **BEAC** (Bank of Central African States): Regional mobile money regulation

### 5.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **No public map of mobile money agent locations** exists. Operators maintain internal registries but do not share them.
- **Agent liquidity data** is not available -- a major pain point for users.
- **Biggest opportunity**: Geolocated map of all mobile money agent points in Bonamoussadi, with brand, status (active/inactive), and potentially crowdsourced liquidity information. This is valuable to operators (for network optimization), regulators (for financial inclusion metrics), and consumers.

---

## VERTICAL 6: PHARMACY

### 6.1 Existing Data Sources

- **DPML** (Direction de la Pharmacie, du Medicament et des Laboratoires): [dpml.cm](https://dpml.cm/) / [dpml.agisoin.com](https://dpml.agisoin.com/index.php/en/) -- Maintains the official marketing authorization (AMM) registry and lists of licensed pharmaceutical establishments, including responsible pharmacist names and locations.
- **CNOPC** (Conseil National de l'Ordre des Pharmaciens du Cameroun): [ordrepharmacien.cm](https://ordrepharmacien.cm/) -- Professional body that registers pharmacists.
- **businesslist.co.cm**: Lists [19+ pharmacies in Douala](https://www.businesslist.co.cm/category/pharmacies/city:douala) (likely incomplete).
- **RentechDigital SmartScraper**: Reports **512 pharmacies in Cameroon** total, with **197 in Littoral region** (which includes Douala). Douala city has **300+ pharmacies**.
- **Pharmacies Cameroun mobile app**: Available on [Google Play](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.touss.wilson.pharmaciescameroun), covers pharmacies in 40+ towns.
- **pharmaciedeshopitaux.com**: [Pharmacy directory](https://pharmaciedeshopitaux.com/index.php/annuaire).

### 6.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

- Bonamoussadi has a relatively high density of pharmacies given its middle-class population.
- **Formal pharmacies**: Licensed, registered with DPML and CNOPC, required to have a qualified pharmacist on-site.
- **Informal drug sellers ("pharmacies de rue")**: Unlicensed vendors selling medications in markets and on streets. This is illegal but widespread.
- **Night pharmacies (pharmacies de garde)**: A rotating duty system exists where certain pharmacies stay open overnight and on holidays. This rotation is published but not always reliably.
- **Opening a pharmacy requires**: DPML authorization, CNOPC registration, MINCOMMERCE business registration, and municipal permits. Requirements documented at [primetimelawoffice.com](https://primetimelawoffice.com/requirements-to-open-a-pharmacy-in-cameroon/).

### 6.3 Update Frequency

- Pharmacy openings/closures: Quarterly to annual churn. Opening a pharmacy involves lengthy regulatory process (months).
- Pharmacie de garde rotations: Weekly or bi-weekly schedules.
- Drug availability: Can change daily due to supply chain issues and import dependencies.
- Inspections: Required at least once annually by law.

### 6.4 Data Quality Risks

- **DPML records may lag reality**: Pharmacies can close or relocate without timely updates to official records.
- **Informal drug sellers** are entirely absent from official databases.
- **Location data**: Official records often use descriptive addresses ("near the roundabout"), not GPS coordinates.
- **Medication availability**: No public real-time tracking of drug stock levels.

### 6.5 Key Local Actors

- **DPML / MINSANTE**: Pharmaceutical regulation
- **CNOPC**: Professional pharmacist body
- **CENAME** (Centrale Nationale d'Approvisionnement en Medicaments): National drug supply
- **LANACOME**: Drug quality control laboratory
- **Private distributors**: Listed in [DPML directory](https://dpml.cm/images/Repertoire/EP/Societes_Distribution_Produits_Pharmaceutiques_23_09_2021.pdf)
- **Maligah.com**: Online pharmacy directory platform

### 6.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **No geolocated pharmacy map with real-time data** (opening hours, de-garde status, drug availability).
- **Informal drug market** is unmapped and a public health concern.
- **Biggest opportunity**: A comprehensive pharmacy layer with GPS coordinates, opening hours, pharmacie-de-garde rotation, and (ideally) drug availability indicators. Public health value is high; commercial value exists for pharmaceutical distributors.

---

## VERTICAL 7: TRANSPORT / ROADS (INCLUDING BLOCKED ROADS)

### 7.1 Existing Data Sources

- **OpenStreetMap**: [Cameroon Roads on HDX](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/hotosm_cmr_roads) -- exportable road network, though incomplete for eastern/southeastern Douala.
- **JICA**: Conducted a [Data Collection Survey on Transport Network Development](https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/12286837_01.pdf) for Cameroon.
- **MobiliseYourCity**: [Support program in Cameroon](https://www.mobiliseyourcity.net/mobiliseyourcitys-support-cameroon) -- urban mobility planning.
- **CUD Plan Directeur d'Urbanisme**: [Master Urban Plan](https://www.fsmtoolbox.com/assets/pdf/Plan_Directeur_d_urbanisme.pdf) -- road network planning document.
- **Yango**: Active ride-hailing service in Douala ([yango.com/en_cm/city/douala](https://yango.com/en_cm/city/douala/)), collects GPS trace data. Uses AI routing that adapts to real-time traffic.
- **Academic research**: GPS-based study of motorcycle taxi activity in Cameroon published in Journal of Transport Geography ([ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0966692318309141)).
- **Cameroon-streets.openalfa.com**: Street-level guides for [Bonamoussadi](https://cameroon-streets.openalfa.com/bonamoussadi) and sub-neighborhoods.

### 7.2 Field Realities in Bonamoussadi

- **Road conditions**: Douala's roads suffer rapid degradation. Only ~10% of Cameroon's roadways are tarred. Even newly constructed roads develop potholes quickly due to poor construction quality. [Cameroon Tribune](https://www.cameroon-tribune.cm/article.html/33809/fr.html/rapid-degradation-of-roads-in-douala).
- **Seasonal flooding**: Douala IV and V (which includes Bonamoussadi) are particularly vulnerable. Floods in June-September block roads, damage infrastructure, and cut off areas. [Flood-prone area maps exist in academic literature](https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Map-of-flood-prone-areas-in-Douala_fig2_273321869).
- **Transport modes**: Moto-taxis ("bendskin") account for **40% of trips**. Estimated 22,000-25,000 moto-taxis in Douala. SOCATUR buses handle less than 1% of trips. Personal vehicles, shared taxis, and Yango ride-hailing fill the rest.
- **Road blocks**: Caused by flooding (seasonal), construction works, accidents, and occasionally political demonstrations. No systematic reporting mechanism exists.
- **Emergency road plan**: Mayor Roger Mbassa Ndine announced emergency road rehabilitation in August 2024 after heavy rain damage.

### 7.3 Update Frequency

- **Road conditions**: Change with seasons. Rainy season (June-September) causes rapid deterioration. Post-rainy season repairs occur gradually.
- **Blocked roads**: Can change hourly during rainy season. Construction blockages last days to months.
- **New road construction**: Major projects (like Yaounde-Douala Highway Phase II, 880 billion FCFA, target 2028) are multi-year.
- **Traffic patterns**: Daily variation. No public real-time traffic data exists aside from Yango/Google Maps routing.

### 7.4 Data Quality Risks

- **OSM road data is static** and not updated for seasonal flooding or construction.
- **No systematic blocked-road reporting** -- information travels by word of mouth and social media.
- **GPS traces from ride-hailing** are private (Yango does not share this data).
- **Informal routes** (unpaved shortcuts through neighborhoods) exist but are not mapped.
- **Address system absence**: Without formal addresses, navigation relies on landmarks, making routing data less structured.

### 7.5 Key Local Actors

- **CUD / Mairie de Douala**: Road maintenance, urban planning
- **MINTP** (Ministry of Public Works): National road infrastructure
- **MINT** (Ministry of Transport): Transport regulation
- **Yango**: Ride-hailing, GPS data collection
- **SOCATUR**: Bus operator
- **Moto-taxi unions/syndicats**: Informal but organized groups with ground knowledge
- **World Bank**: Funds urban transport and infrastructure projects

### 7.6 Gaps and Opportunities

- **No real-time or near-real-time road condition / blocked road data** exists publicly.
- **Flood-risk overlay** on road maps does not exist in an operational, consumer-facing format.
- **Moto-taxi route patterns** are unstudied outside of a few academic papers.
- **Biggest opportunity**: A dynamic road condition layer that combines static road network data (from OSM) with crowdsourced or sensor-based updates on road blockages, flooding, and construction. During rainy season, this is life-or-death information for commuters. Value extends to logistics companies, emergency services, and urban planners.

---

## SUMMARY OF GAPS AND STRATEGIC OPPORTUNITIES

| Vertical | Key Gap | Opportunity Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Billboards** | No georeferenced inventory; no content tracking | High (advertising industry, municipal tax enforcement) |
| **Alcohol** | Informal drinking spots unmapped; no retail point registry | High (SABC, Guinness, new entrants) |
| **Census** | Last census 2005; no neighborhood-level data | Very High (government, health, commercial planning) |
| **Fuel** | No real-time station status or availability data | High (consumers, fuel companies) |
| **Mobile Money** | No public agent location map; no liquidity data | Very High (operators, regulators, consumers) |
| **Pharmacy** | No geolocated directory with hours/garde status | High (public health, pharmaceutical distributors) |
| **Transport/Roads** | No real-time road condition or blocked-road data | Very High (commuters, logistics, emergency services) |

**Cross-cutting insight**: The fundamental enabler across all verticals is **geolocated, regularly updated field data** -- something that neither government agencies nor the private sector currently provide for Bonamoussadi or Douala at the required granularity. The combination of high mobile penetration (96.4%), growing smartphone adoption, and the absence of any comprehensive urban data platform creates a clear market opening.

**Regulatory timeline note**: The June 23, 2026 compliance deadline for Law No. 2024/017 (data protection) means any data collection methodology must be designed with privacy-by-design principles from the start.

---
